Publishing an Angular Web Application to GitHub Pages

Angular
Radek Šťasta
null

Introduction

When creating websites, a necessary requirement is to publish them on the internet so that they are accessible to the world at a chosen web address. For these purposes, there are many web hosting providers that offer services both free and for a fee. In earlier times, the classic scenario of creating a website was such that a person usually created a multi-page website (i.e., a website consisting of individual HTML pages like Home, Contact, etc.), paid for a domain, and uploaded the necessary files to this domain. Nowadays, it is common to have a single-page web application (created, for example, in frameworks like Angular, React, Vue). Of course, it depends on the use case. Both approaches have their pros and cons. Moreover, it is now common to have the source code backed up by a version control tool such as Git. The GitHub web service offers this version control for free (for basic use) and also offers the GitHub Pages service, which this article focuses on.
GitHub Pages offers the same thing as other hosting providers: publishing web pages under the project's web address on GitHub. In this article, we will specifically focus on how to create an Angular web application and publish it on the web using GitHub Pages. The advantage of this approach is the fact that you have the source code of the web application stored in a project on GitHub, from which the final website is created using, for example, the angular-cli-ghpages package and is versioned in the same GitHub project as its source files.

Requirements

  • An account on GitHub, where the project will be created and subsequently published through GitHub Pages
  • Installed version control tool Git
  • Installed Node.js and npm (Node Package Manager)
  • Basic understanding of working with the command line in the chosen operating system. This guide was created in Linux, but the commands should not differ in Windows.

    • Guide to Publishing an Angular Application to GitHub Pages

    Creating a Single-Page Angular Web Application

    First, we will create a simple single-page web application using Angular CLI. This tool allows us to create a simple project in Angular and also creates a local Git repository for this project. If Angular CLI is not available on the system, it can be installed using the command: 
    npm install -g @angular/cli
    We create a new application in the current directory with the command: 
    ng new my-website
    After running the command, a simple guide will appear to create a new application. For this guide, it doesn't matter which settings are chosen. For the CSS format, you can choose CSS, and when asked about enabling SSR and SSG, select No. Instead of my-website, you can choose any other name for the application.
    Now we can test that our new application works using the command ng serve in the application directory: 
    cd my-website # or another chosen application name
ng serve --open
    If everything went well, a web browser should open with the content of the demo application.
    Demo application served on localhost

    Creating a Repository on GitHub

    Now that we have a prepared web application, it is necessary to create a repository on GitHub where we will first upload the source code and then generate the compiled files that will be used for publishing through GitHub Pages.
    Creating new repository on GitHub
    If we want the web pages to be directly available under the user account's address, we need to create a repository in the form [username].github.io (e.g., as shown in the image above, the pages will be subsequently available at radek-stasta-dev.github.io). If we choose any other repository name, it will be available at [username].github.io/[repository name]. After creating the repository, we need to note down the repository address with the .git extension, which we will need in the next step.
    Place where to find repository URL on GitHub

    Creating a Personal Access Token on GitHub

    If you already have an access token or if you upload to GitHub using another method (via SSH, GitHub CLI, VS Code extension...), you can skip this step.
    To upload files to GitHub, a personal access token can be used. This token will be used to authorize the account uploading the files when uploading local files to GitHub. To create an access token, click on the profile picture on GitHub and then select Settings. In the left column with the menu select Developer Settings at the bottom and in the next menu Personal access tokens and the Fine-grained token option. Click on the Generate new token button.
    Fill in the token name and select the All repositories option for repository access (or Only select repositories if we want to restrict which repositories we set permissions for). Then in the Repository permissions section, set the Contents permission to Access: Read and Write and press the Generate token button.
    Personal access token basic settings
    Personal access token repository permissions settings
    On the next screen, the generated access token will be displayed, and it is NECESSARY to copy and save this access token because it cannot be displayed again for security reasons. It would be necessary to generate the access token again.
    Generated personal access token

    Uploading Local Files to the GitHub Repository

    As already mentioned, Angular CLI automatically created a local Git repository for our application. Now it is necessary to link this repository with the newly created repository on GitHub. The following commands run in the Angular project directory will help us with this (the git config commands are only necessary if you don't have a username and email for Git already set globally): 
    git remote add origin https://github.com/[username]/[repository name].git
git config --global user.name "[username]"
git config --global user.email "[email]"
git commit -m "[commit message]"
git push -u origin master
    A specific example might look like this: 
    git remote add origin https://github.com/radek-stasta-dev/radek-stasta-dev.github.io.git
git config --global user.name "Radek Šťasta"
git config --global user.email "rstasta@icloud.com"
git commit -m "Initial commit"
git push -u origin master
    After calling the git push command, it is necessary to fill in the username for GitHub access (email) and the password. For the password, it is necessary to fill in (copy) the personal access token, as GitHub has not supported uploading files via the command line using a username and password since 2021. If everything went well, we can check the newly uploaded files in the GitHub interface.
    Uploaded application files in repository on GitHub

    Creating Compiled Pages for Publication Using the angular-cli-ghpages Package

    To simplify the compilation of the application's source code into static files that can be published to GitHub Pages, we will use the angular-cli-ghpages package. This package will compile our source files and create a new branch in the repository, where the resulting files will be uploaded. First, it is necessary to add this package to the application using the Angular CLI command (in the application directory): 
    ng add angular-cli-ghpages
    We can also upload this change to the repository using: 
    git add -A
git commit -m "Added angular-cli-ghpages"
git push
    Now, just run the following command in the application directory, and angular-cli-ghpages will take care of everything else: 
    ng deploy --repo=https://github.com/[username]/[repository name].git --base-href=/
    Example of angular-cli-ghpages deploy output
    If everything went well, angular-cli-ghpages created a new gh-pages branch on GitHub, where it uploaded the compiled web page files.
    Uploaded compiled files in gh-pages branch on GitHub

    Setting the branch with files for GitHub Pages on GitHub

    The last step is to set in GitHub which branch should be used for GitHub Pages. In the top tabs in the repository, select Settings and then Pages in the left menu. In the default settings, the master branch is used, which needs to be switched to the newly created gh-pages branch and press the Save button.
    GitHub Pages settings
    GitHub will register this change after a while, switch the branches, and if we click on the Visit site button at the top of the screen or open the browser at the web page address, the same pages as those we tested locally should be displayed, now published on the web via GitHub Pages.
    Final published application on GitHub using GitHub Pages

    Conclusion

    In this guide, we showed how to create a basic Angular application via Angular CLI and how to publish this application on the web using GitHub Pages. This approach has its limitations — the most important being the 1 GB file limit for GitHub Pages a nd the limit of 10 builds per hour. However, this should suffice for private purposes in most cases. A typical workflow is to make local changes to the source files in the master branch, where we can create dozens of commits and then, when we are satisfied with the result, run the ng deploy command, which compiles the source files and deploys the new website. And yes, those who are observant will surely notice that these pages are also published on the web in exactly this way.
    Comments
    Summary
    Introduction
    Requirements
    Guide to Publishing an Angular Application to GitHub Pages
    Creating a Single-Page Angular Web Application
    Creating a Repository on GitHub
    Creating a Personal Access Token on GitHub
    Uploading Local Files to the GitHub Repository
    Creating Compiled Pages for Publication Using the angular-cli-ghpages Package
    Setting the branch with files for GitHub Pages on GitHub
    Conclusion
    Comments
